section.title: Peter's Confession
section.sectionRationale: This is the hinge of the Gospel. At Caesarea Philippi Peter confesses Jesus as the Christ, the Son of the living God, and Jesus begins to reveal that the Christ must suffer, die, and rise. To follow this Messiah is to take up a cross.
section.thesis: Jesus is the Christ, the Son of the living God, confessed by revelation and destined to build His church; yet He is a crucified Messiah, and those who would follow Him must deny themselves, take up their cross, and lose their lives to find them.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the confession at Caesarea Philippi and the promise to build the church; the first passion prediction and Peter's rebuke; and the call to take up the cross and follow.

verses.text: When Jesus came to the region of Caesarea Philippi, He questioned His disciples: “Who do people say the Son of Man is?”
They replied, “Some say John the Baptist; others say Elijah; and still others, Jeremiah or one of the prophets.”
“But what about you?” Jesus asked. “Who do you say I am?”
Simon Peter answered, “You are the {{christos|Christ}}, the Son of the living God.”
Jesus replied, “Blessed are you, Simon son of Jonah! For this was not revealed to you by flesh and blood, but by My Father in heaven.
And I tell you that you are Peter, and on this {{petra|rock}} I will build My {{ekklesia|church}}, and the gates of Hades will not prevail against it.
I will give you the keys of the kingdom of heaven. Whatever you bind on earth will be bound in heaven, and whatever you loose on earth will be loosed in heaven.”
Then He admonished the disciples not to tell anyone that He was the Christ.

From that time on Jesus began to show His disciples that He must go to Jerusalem and suffer many things at the hands of the elders, chief priests, and scribes, and that He must be killed and on the third day be raised to life.
Peter took Him aside and began to rebuke Him. “Far be it from You, Lord!” he said. “This shall never happen to You!”
But Jesus turned and said to Peter, “Get behind Me, Satan! You are a stumbling block to Me. For you do not have in mind the things of God, but the things of men.”

units.label: Take up your cross
units.note: lose your life to find it

verses.text: Then Jesus told His disciples, “If anyone wants to come after Me, he must deny himself and take up his cross and follow Me.
For whoever wants to save his life will lose it, but whoever loses his life for My sake will find it.
What will it profit a man if he gains the whole world, yet forfeits his {{psyche|soul}}? Or what can a man give in exchange for his soul?
For the Son of Man will come in His Father’s glory with His angels, and then He will repay each one according to what he has done.
Truly I tell you, some who are standing here will not taste death before they see the Son of Man coming in His kingdom.”

christos.gloss: The Anointed One, the Messiah, God's promised King. Peter's confession names Jesus not merely a prophet but the Christ, the Son of the living God, the one all Scripture anticipates.

petra.gloss: A rock or bedrock, a massive foundation stone, distinct from petros, a stone or boulder, which is Peter's name. The wordplay links Peter, the confessing apostle, to the rock on which Christ builds, the confessed truth that Jesus is the Christ.

ekklesia.gloss: An assembly, the gathered people, the church. Jesus calls it My church, His own new-covenant community, which He Himself will build and which death itself cannot overpower.

psyche.gloss: Life or soul, the whole self that lives. To lose one's psyche for Christ is to find it; to gain the world at its cost is the ruin of everything, for no price can buy it back.

basic.context: At Caesarea Philippi Jesus asks His disciples who people say He is, then who they say He is. Peter answers, You are the Christ, the Son of the living God. Jesus blesses him, promises to build His church, and gives the keys of the kingdom. Then He begins to teach that the Christ must suffer, die, and rise. Peter rebukes Him and is sharply corrected. Following this Messiah, Jesus says, means denying yourself and taking up your cross.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the turning point of the Gospel: the identity of Jesus is settled, and from here the road runs to the cross. The confession is right, but Peter's idea of the Christ is wrong, which is why Jesus at once redefines messiahship as suffering. Note the debated meaning of the rock and the keys, and the puzzling promise that some will see the Son of Man coming before they die.
basic.originalLanguages: Peter confesses Jesus as the Christos, the anointed King. Jesus plays on names: Peter, petros, a stone, and the petra, the bedrock, on which He builds His ekklesia, His church. And He warns that gaining the world while forfeiting the psyche, the soul or life, is total loss.
basic.typology: Jesus building His church on the rock echoes God as the Rock of Israel and the temple built on a foundation stone. The suffering and rising Christ fulfills the pattern of the righteous sufferer and the servant of Isaiah 53.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Christ, the Son of the living God, and He is the crucified and risen Christ. He builds an indestructible church, holds the keys of the kingdom, and calls His people to follow Him in the way of the cross before He comes in glory.
basic.god: The Father reveals the Son to Peter; saving knowledge is God's gift, not human insight. God's way of victory runs through suffering, overturning human expectations, and He will finally repay each person according to what they have done.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 53 foretells the suffering servant. Psalm 118 speaks of the rejected stone become the cornerstone. Matthew 18 repeats the binding and loosing to the church. Romans 1 confesses Jesus as Son of God in power by the resurrection.
basic.oneStory: The Gospel turns here: Jesus is confessed as the Christ, and the Christ is revealed as one who must die and rise. The church is founded on this confession, and the way of discipleship is the way of the cross. The whole story now bends toward Jerusalem, where the Son of the living God will lay down His life and rise, building a church the gates of death cannot overcome.

context.literary: Caesarea Philippi marks the great hinge of Matthew: the phrase from that time on Jesus began signals a decisive turn from Galilean ministry toward the passion in Jerusalem. Peter's confession answers the question the whole first half has raised, and the passion prediction, Peter's rebuke, and the cross-bearing call form a tight unit that reframes what Messiah and discipleship mean.
context.historical: Caesarea Philippi lay in the far north, a center of pagan worship near a shrine to Pan and a temple to Caesar, a pointed backdrop for confessing the true King and Son of God. Popular opinion cast Jesus as a returning prophet. The gates of a city were where its power and counsel sat; the gates of Hades picture the power of death.
context.canonical: The confession draws on the messianic hope of the Psalms and Prophets and on Daniel 7's Son of Man. The suffering Christ fulfills Isaiah 53. The rejected-stone imagery recalls Psalm 118:22, applied to Christ throughout the New Testament. Binding and loosing reappears in Matthew 18:18 and informs the church's authority in Acts.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the decisive confession of Jesus as the Christ, to redefine His messiahship as suffering and resurrection, and to lay down the cost of discipleship as self-denial and cross-bearing in hope of His coming glory.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The confession and its blessing describe a unique moment, yet they prescribe the confession every believer must make. The call to take up the cross is directly prescriptive for all who would follow Jesus, not a burden for a few.

debates.question: What is the rock on which Christ builds His church?

views.label: Peter as the confessing apostle
views.case: Jesus addresses Peter directly, names him rock, and gives him the keys; the apostles, with Peter foremost at Pentecost, are the foundation on which the church is built, Ephesians 2:20.

views.label: Peter's confession of Christ
views.case: The wordplay distinguishes petros, a stone, from petra, bedrock; the foundation is the truth Peter just confessed, that Jesus is the Christ, so the church rests on Christ Himself and the apostolic testimony to Him.

debates.bearing: Either way the church is Christ's own building, founded on the apostolic confession of Him; the promise magnifies Jesus the builder, not Peter as an independent authority.

debates.question: What does Jesus mean that some will not taste death before they see the Son of Man coming in His kingdom?

views.label: The transfiguration soon to follow
views.case: The very next scene, six days later, gives Peter, James, and John a preview of the Son of Man in glory, a near fulfillment fitting some standing here.

views.label: The resurrection, Pentecost, and the kingdom's advance
views.case: The resurrection and the Spirit-empowered spread of the gospel, and for many the fall of Jerusalem in judgment, display the Son of Man's reign within that generation, fulfilling the promise on a larger scale.

debates.bearing: Both keep the saying honest to Jesus' own generation; the transfiguration, resurrection, and inbreaking kingdom together vindicate His words without deferring them beyond those who heard.

study.originalLanguages: Peter names Jesus the Christos, the Anointed One, the Son of the living God, a confession Jesus says came by the Father's revelation. Jesus answers with a play on names: you are Petros, a stone, and on this petra, the bedrock, I will build My ekklesia, My assembly, which death's gates cannot overpower. Yet the Christ must suffer, and when Peter refuses this, Jesus warns that to gain the whole world while forfeiting the psyche, the soul or true self, is to lose everything, for nothing can be given in exchange for it. The name, the rock, and the soul all turn on rightly grasping who this Messiah is and what following Him costs.

typology.fulfillment: God the Rock of Israel and the cornerstone of Zion, fulfilled in Christ on whom the church is built, Psalm 118:22, 1 Peter 2:4-6.
The servant of Isaiah 53 who is killed and then exalted, fulfilled in the crucified and risen Christ.

typology.type: The Son of Man coming in His kingdom
typology.fulfillment: Daniel 7's Son of Man given dominion, realized in Jesus' resurrection, reign, and return, Daniel 7:13-14.

study.christ: Jesus is the Christ, the Son of the living God, and this confession is the rock-truth on which He builds a church the gates of Hades cannot overcome. But He is a crucified Christ. From this turning point He sets His face toward Jerusalem to suffer at the hands of the leaders, to be killed, and on the third day to rise. He rebukes even Peter for a messiahship without a cross, for the things of men would spare Him the very cross by which He saves. He calls His followers into the same pattern, self-denial and cross-bearing now, glory when He comes with His Father and His angels. To know Him truly is to follow Him to the cross and beyond it to life.
study.god: The Father Himself reveals the Son to Peter, so that true confession of Christ is God's gift and not the achievement of flesh and blood. God's way to victory runs, astonishingly, through the suffering and death of His Son, a path so contrary to human expectation that Peter recoils and is rebuked. God will build His church and preserve it against death itself, He entrusts the keys of the kingdom to His people, and He will finally repay each one according to what they have done when the Son of Man comes in glory.

crossReferences.note: The suffering servant despised, killed, and then vindicated, behind the passion prediction.
The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone, applied to Christ the church's foundation.
Binding and loosing given to the church, extending the authority named here.
The Son of Man given everlasting dominion, behind His coming in His kingdom.

study.oneStory: Peter's confession is the great hinge of the Gospel: Jesus is the Christ, the Son of the living God, and the Christ must die and rise. On this confession the church is founded, unassailable by death, and to follow this Messiah is to walk the way of the cross toward the glory of His coming. From here the whole story bends toward Jerusalem, where the Son of the living God lays down His life and takes it up again, so that all who lose their lives for His sake will find them.

quiz.q: How did Peter come to confess Jesus as the Christ?

options.text: It was revealed to him by the Father in heaven, not by flesh and blood.
options.note: Verse 17 grounds the confession in God's revelation, not human insight.

options.text: He reasoned it out from all the miracles he had seen.
options.note: The miracles pointed to it, but Jesus says the confession came by the Father's revelation, not mere reasoning.

options.text: He simply repeated what the crowds were saying about Jesus.
options.note: The crowds said prophet or Elijah; Peter's confession went beyond them by revelation.

options.text: He was told to say it by the other disciples.
options.note: The confession was Peter's own, given by the Father, not coached by the others.

quiz.q: How does Jesus redefine what it means to be the Christ?

options.text: The Christ must suffer, be killed, and on the third day rise again.
options.note: Verse 21 reveals a suffering and rising Messiah, overturning expectations.

options.text: The Christ will go to Jerusalem to establish His rule.
options.note: He does go to Jerusalem, but to suffer and die first, which this omits.

options.text: The Christ will avoid all suffering by God's protection.
options.note: This is Peter's error, which Jesus sharply rebukes.

options.text: The Christ will conquer Rome by military force.
options.note: Jesus rejects a political-military messiahship; His victory comes through the cross.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus rebuke Peter so sharply after praising him?

options.text: Peter opposed the cross, setting his mind on human things rather than God's.
options.note: Verse 23 names Peter a stumbling block for minding the things of men.

options.text: Peter spoke without waiting to be asked.
options.note: The problem was not his boldness but the substance, resisting God's plan of the cross.

options.text: Peter doubted that Jesus was the Christ.
options.note: Peter had just confessed Him; his error was about the way of the Christ, not His identity.

options.text: Peter wanted Jesus to suffer more than necessary.
options.note: The opposite; Peter wanted to spare Jesus the cross altogether.

quiz.q: What does Jesus say it costs to follow Him?

options.text: Self-denial, taking up one's cross, and losing one's life for His sake to find it.
options.note: Verses 24-25 lay out the way of the cross for every follower.

options.text: A willingness to give up some comforts for His cause.
options.note: It involves sacrifice, but Jesus asks for the whole self, not just some comforts.

options.text: Gaining as much of the world as possible for the kingdom.
options.note: Jesus warns that gaining the world at the cost of the soul is ruin, the reverse of this.

options.text: Nothing at all; following Jesus is without cost.
options.note: Jesus explicitly names the cost as cross-bearing and self-denial.

quiz.q: What is promised about the church Jesus builds?

options.text: Jesus Himself builds it on the confession of Christ, and the gates of Hades will not prevail against it.
options.note: Verse 18 promises an indestructible church built by Christ.

options.text: The church will be strong as long as its leaders are faithful.
options.note: Leaders matter, but the promise rests on Christ the builder, not on human faithfulness.

options.text: The church will never face any opposition or death.
options.note: Death and opposition come, but cannot overpower it; this overstates the promise.

options.text: The church depends entirely on Peter's personal authority.
options.note: It is Christ's church, built by Him; the promise magnifies the builder, not Peter alone.

questions: Jesus asks everyone the same question: who do you say I am? How would your honest daily life answer that question this week?
Peter confessed rightly yet resisted the cross. Where are you tempted to want a Christ of glory without the way of the cross?
Jesus says whoever loses his life for His sake will find it. What would it look like to lose your life for Him in one concrete area right now?
The whole world is not worth the soul. Where are you tempted to trade something eternal for something passing?
True confession of Christ is a gift of the Father. How does that move you from pride in your faith to gratitude and prayer for others?

translationNotes.note: There is a wordplay in Greek between Petros, Peter, meaning a stone, and petra, meaning bedrock; the church is built on the rock, whether understood as Peter the confessor or the confession of Christ, and in either case by Christ the builder.
The gates of Hades pictures the power and realm of death; the promise is that death itself cannot overpower the church Christ builds.
Binding and loosing was rabbinic language for declaring what is forbidden or permitted, and for the authority to include or exclude; the same authority is given to the wider church in Matthew 18:18.
